coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive colorimetric assay melamine based on in situ reduction to formation of CQDs-silver nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wei-Feng
Qiang, Yin
Meng, Xian-Hua
Yang, Jun-Li
Shi, Yan-Ping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the in situ reduction of Ag+ by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to the formation of CQDs-silver nanocomposite (CQDs-silver NPs) was exploited to develop a facile and ultrasensitive colorimetric sensor platform. Due to the high affinity between Ag and melamine, melamine could significantly induce the well-dispersed CQDs-silver NPs aggregation. Consequently, the solution color of CQDs-silver NPs changed from bright yellow to red. Based on this finding, we proposed a new colorimetric method to qualitatively and quantitatively detect melamine by the naked eyes and UV–vis spectroscopy, respectively. Under the optimum conditions, the linearity of melamine with the solution absorbance (ΔR) was plotted in the concentration range of 1 × 10−10–1 × 10−8 g/mL (y = 0.55 + 0.049x, R = 0.9596) and 2.5 × 10−8–5 × 10−7 g/mL (y = 3.43 + 0.42x, R = 0.9961). The developed method showed excellent detection sensitivity for melamine with the detection limit as low as 7.9 × 10−12 g/mL (eq. 65.3 pmol L−1). Finally, the developed method was employed to detect the amount of melamine in cow milk and milk powderand a recovery of 87.6–116%, and RSD of 1.6–3.9% was achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasensitive
Colorimetric detection
Melamine
CQDs-silver nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs-silver NPs
The mechanism of the colorimetric sensor
Analytical performance of the colorimetric system
The analysis of melamine in the milk sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81673325 , 81711540311 , and 21705156 ), and the CAS Pioneer Hundred Talents Program .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the colorimetric sensing of melamine based on in situ formation of CQDs-silver NPs.
UV–vis spectra of CQDs and the as-synthesized CQDs-silver NPs. The inset photo tubes are respectively CODs (a) and CQDs-silver NPs (b).

(A) Absorption spectra of CQDs-silver NPs in the absence and presence of melamine, the inset photo tubes are correspond to the absence (a) and presence (b) of melamine. (B-D) TEM images of CQDs-silver NPs; B, the TEM image of CQDs-silver NPs in the absence of melamine; C and D were the TEM images with different magnification after addition of melamine. All the concentration of melamine was 1 × 10−7 g/mL.
The effect of different conditions on the in situ synthesis of the CQDs-silver NPs.

Response of CQDs-silver NPs to melamine with different concentration. (A) Absorption spectra changes with the addition of different concentration melamine (1 × 10−10, 2.5 × 10−10, 5 × 10−10, 1 × 10−9, 2.5 × 10−9, 5 × 10−9, 1 × 10−8, 2.5 × 10−8, 5 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7, 2.5 × 10−7, 5 × 10−7 g/mL); (B) Plot of absorbance changes at 405 nm vs concentration of melamine.

The photo (A) and relative absorbance (B) of the CQDs-silver NPs solution in the presence of different substances. Substances: 1, alanine; 2, lysine; 3, valine; 4, histidine; 5, glycine; 6, methionine; 7, phenylalanine; 8, ornithine; 9, arginine; 10, threonine; 11, serine; 12, isoleucine; 13, cystine; 14, tyrosine; 15, cysteine; 16, tryptophan; 17, leucine; 18, melamine; 19, blank. The concentration of melamine used was 5 × 10−8 g/mL. The concentration of cystine, cysteine, and histidine were 10 folds higher than the melamine and other substances used were 1000 folds higher than the melamine.

UV–vis spectra as-synthesized CQDs-silver NPs in the addition of different concentration of melamine. The inset photo tubes: (a), CQDs-silver NPs, (b), CQDs-silver NPs + 2.5 × 10−8 g/mL melamine, (c), CQDs-silver NPs + 5 × 10−8 g/mL melamine, and (d), CQDs-silver NPs + 1 × 10−7 g/mL melamine.
Comparison of different Ag-based nanomaterials for melamine detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abs, absorbance; FL, fluorescence; CTA, chromotropic acid; LDH, layered double hydroxides.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of melamine in cow milk and milk powder samples.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive colorimetric assay melamine based on in situ reduction to formation of CQDs-silver nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources, Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources
Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the in situ reduction of Ag+ by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to the formation of CQDs-silver nanocomposite (CQDs-silver NPs) was exploited to develop a facile and ultrasensitive colorimetric sensor platform. Due to the high affinity between Ag and melamine, melamine could significantly induce the well-dispersed CQDs-silver NPs aggregation. Consequently, the solution color of CQDs-silver NPs changed from bright yellow to red. Based on this finding, we proposed a new colorimetric method to qualitatively and quantitatively detect melamine by the naked eyes and UV–vis spectroscopy, respectively. Under the optimum conditions, the linearity of melamine with the solution absorbance (ΔR) was plotted in the concentration range of 1 × 10−10–1 × 10−8 g/mL (y = 0.55 + 0.049x, R = 0.9596) and 2.5 × 10−8–5 × 10−7 g/mL (y = 3.43 + 0.42x, R = 0.9961). The developed method showed excellent detection sensitivity for melamine with the detection limit as low as 7.9 × 10−12 g/mL (eq. 65.3 pmol L−1). Finally, the developed method was employed to detect the amount of melamine in cow milk and milk powderand a recovery of 87.6–116%, and RSD of 1.6–3.9% was achieved.

ce:section.ce:para: Metallic nanoparticle-based colorimetric sensing system has attracted tremendous attention due to its visibility, simplicity, high sensitivity, and good biocompatibility [1–5]. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are one of the most common metallic nanoparticles, and possess tunable optical properties, i.e. local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR). Typically, spherical AgNPs with the size less than 10 nm show a characteristic LSPR absorption around 390 nm [6]. This absorption wavelength for AgNPs closely relies on the size and shape of nanoparticles, and when the nanoparticle size increases, a red shift will occur [7,8]. Based on this unique optical property, AgNPs have been explored as novel visible colorimetric reagents for developing several elegant sensors and biosensors [9–12].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used in bioimaging, photovoltaics, photocatalysis, electrode materials, and biosensing due to their excellent physicochemical, optical, and electronic properties. Recently, CQDs showed the potential as reducing agents for synthesis of metal nanoparticles [13–18]. Shen et al. [13] described the synthesis of Ag NPs, which used carbon dots as reducing and stable agent. The prepared Ag NPs were then exploited for the detection of Ag+. Zhang et al. [14] used CQDs as reductant and stabilizer for the growth of Au-Ag bimetallic nanocomposite, and based on the prepared nanocomposite, they developed an optical probe for the assay of hydrogen peroxide and lactate. Ma et al. [16] developed a C-dots/AgNPs based fluorescence sensor for quantitative analysis of H2O2 and glucose. Lu et al. [18] used graphitic carbon nitride nanodots as a reductant for the synthesis of AgNPs and explored a colorimetric platform for biothiols biosensing.

Melamine is a common chemical material that has been widely used in industries of fomaldehyde resins, fire-retardant materials, and pesticides [19,20]. However, owing to its high nitrogen content (66.6% by mass) and low cost, melamine is illegally added to milk, infant formula, and pet food to apparently increase its protein level [21]. In addition, the migration of melamine from tableware and packaging materials into food is highly possible [22,23]. The excessive intake of melamine can result in kidney stones, renal failure, and even death in humans. In European Union, the specific migration limit for melamine was strictly set as 2.5 mg/kg. Up to now, series of techniques have been employed for the detection of melamine, such as HPLC [24], electrospray ionization-high resolution mass spectrometry [25], electrochemiluminescence [26], colorimetric method [27], capillary electrophoresis [28], and electrochemical methods [29]. Nevertheless, facile, highly sensitive, and feasible approaches for the convenient detection of melamine are still highly desirable.

In this study, the in situ reduction Ag+ by CQDs to formation of CQDs-silver NPs was exploited to develop a facile and ultrasensitive colorimetric method for melamine detection. The detection mechanism is schematically shown in Scheme 1 . Melamine can significantly aggregate the well-monodispersed CQDs-silver NPs, which could result in the solution color changing from bright yellow to red. Comparison to other method, the proposed colorimetric method showed several advantages on melamine detection, including facile operation, no need for sophisticated instruments, high sensitivity, and good selectivity. Furthermore, the detection and discrimination process can be observed via the naked eye. Herein, the work reported the construction of CQDs-silver NPs and its application to qualitatively and quantitatively detect melamine, as well its successfully application in real samples.

AgNO3 and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai. China). Amino acids were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Analytical grade CHCl3, HCl, and methanol were supplied by Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Melamine was purchased from Sigma (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used as received without further treatment. The deionized water was prepared by Lakecore ultrapure water system (OKP-S210, Shanghai laikie instrument Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China) and used throughout the experiment.

UV–vis spectra were recorded by an instrument of UV–vis spectra analyzer (PerkinElmer, USA). The detection of melamine was carried out on an enzyme analyzer, LeiDu RT-6100, (Shenzhen. China). The optical photographs were taken by a Huawei mobile phone digital camera (Shenzhen. China). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to monitor CQDs-silver NPs, which was carried out on an H-7500 (Hitachi, Japan) operating at a 80 kV accelerated voltage. Constant temperature incubator shaker SPH-200D (Shanghai Shi-ping, China) was also used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs-silver NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment using of dried Lily flower according to the Ref. [30]. Briefly, 0.5 g of dry Lily flower was powdered by a mortar. The obtained Lily flower powder and 0.5 mL ultrapure water were transferred into a 20 mL Telfon equipped stainless steel autoclave, and maintained at 120 °C for 2 h. The obtained black solution was diluted to 20 mL, and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 1 h (repeat three times). Afterwards, the supernatants were spiral flash dried to obtain the solid CQDs.

The CQDs-silver NPs was in situ synthesized as the following procedure. First, AgNO3 (2.0 μL, 10 mmol L−1), CQDs (20 μL, 0.73 mg/mL), and 138 μL ultrapure water were added into each tube. Afterwards, 40 μL NaOH (0.1 mol L−1) was added into the above tube and the obtained solution was incubated at 50 °C for 30 min to grow the CQDs-silver NPs. The obtained bright yellow solution of CQD-silver nanocomposites remained unchanged after two months in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

AgNO3 (2.0 μL, 10 mmol L−1), CQDs (20 μL, 0.73 mg/mL), different amount of melamine, and a specific volume of ultrapure water was added into each tube. 40 μL NaOH (0.1 mol L−1) was then added into the above tube to start the in situ synthesis of CQDs-silver NPs. The total volume in each tube was 200 μL. After maintaining the reaction at 50 °C for 30 min, the absorbance of the solutions was recorded at 405 nm. All the tests were repeated in triplicate. For the selectivity and recovery assays, the procedures were the same as the melamine detection.

Cow milk and milk powder were purchased from the local market and were prepared as the described in the reference with some changes [31]. Typically, 2.00 g cow milk or milk powder was placed into a 15.0 mL centrifuge tube, and 10.0 mL of a solvent mixture (2 mol L−1 HCl/CHCl3, V:V = 1:1) was introduced. After 30 min of sonication, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatants were filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon filter, and then kept at 4 °C for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs-silver NPs was prepared by in situ reduction of Ag+ in a CQDs alkaline solution. Fig. 1 showed the absorption spectra of CQDs and the CQDs-silver NPs. In the absence of Ag+, the CQDs solution showed a low absorbance from 300 to 750 nm (black line). After the addition of Ag+ into the CQDs solution, a new absorption peak at 405 nm appeared (red line), which was consistent with the reference of Ag nanoparticles absorption band [6]. The inset photos showed the solution color of CQDs and CQDs-silver NPs. The CQDs-silver NPs were light yellow, however, CQDs is almost colorless and transparent. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission photo of CQDs and CQDs-silver NPs revealed that the fluorescence emission of CQDs was completely quenched (Fig. S1). This result can be explained by an inner filter effect originating from the large overlap of the CQDs-sliver NPs absorption spectrum with excitation and emission spectra of CQDs (Fig. S2). In addition, TEM and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were employed to characterize the as-prepared nanocomposite (Fig. S3 and S4). The results indicated that the prepared CQDs-silver NPs were monodispersed sphere about 5 nm in size and the characterized EDX peak of Ag could be observed from the EDX spectrum. All the results above demonstrated the successfully preparation of CQDs-silver NPs.

UV–vis spectrum of the sensor system was recorded to confirm the colorimetric detection mechanism of melamine (Fig. 2 A). After addition of melamine into the CQDs-silver NPs solution, the typical absorption peak at 405 nm decreased and the maximum absorption peak shifted to the longer wavelength. Simultaneously, the absorbance from 500 to 750 nm showed a remarkable increase belonging to enhanced scattering due to CQDs-silver NPs aggregation. Fig. 2A inset photos showed the CQDs-silver NPs color change before and after the melamine addition into the sensor system. By comparing the photos, color change from light yellow to red in the solution of CQDs-silver NPs can be easily observed after melamine addition, which also demonstrated an aggregation tuning color changes.

To further confirm the detection mechanism, TEM was employed to characterize the morphology and the size of the CODs-silver nanocomposite. Fig. 2B displayed the TEM image for CQDs-silver NPs, revealed the CQDs-silver NPs as well monodispersed spheres with a size of about 5 nm. Fig. 2C showed the TEM images for the CQDs-silver NPs after addition of melamine and indicated that the monodisperse CQDs-silver NPs was significantly aggregated. This aggregation was attributed to the high affinity between Ag and melamine. To clearly observe the morphology of the aggregated CQDs-silver NPs, Fig. 2D depicted the image with different magnifications. By comparing the sizes of the nanocomposite in Fig. 2B–D, we found that the nanocomposite sizes did not change before and after melamine addition. These results confirmed that the red shift occurred due to the aggregation induced by melamine introduction.

Accordingly, several parameters affecting on experimental performance were investigated, including CQDs amount, AgNO3 concentration, NaOH concentration, reaction time, and reaction temperature (Fig. 3 ).

As a reductant CQDs directly affected the formation of CQDs-silver NPs. Consequently, different volumes of a 7.3 mg/mL CQDs solution were used to test the influence of CQDs on CQDs-silver NPs formation. As illustrated in Fig. 3A, the absorbance of the nanocomposite solution at 405 nm increased with the addition of CQDs, suggesting an increase in nanocomposite generation. When CQDs solution was 20 μL, the absorbance remained constant.

Keeping the CQDs amount constant, the concentration of AgNO3 was discussed ranging from 0.25 to 400 μmol L−1. As Fig. 3B, the system absorbance gradually increased with increasing amounts of Ag+ and showed linearity in the concentrations ranging from 0.25 to 30 μmol L−1. Higher concentration of Ag+, stronger absorbance at 405 nm appeared. For detection feasibility, the concentration of Ag+ was fixed at 30 μmol L−1.

The formation rate of CQDs-silver NPs can be adjusted by the pH with the addition of 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH. The solution absorbance at 405 nm was plotted against the NaOH concentration (Fig. 3C). As shown, the optimized NaOH concentration ranged from 20 to 40 mmol L−1, and correspondingly, the formed nanocomposite had the maximum absorbance at 405 nm. Considering the solution pH could be easily affected by sample matrix, and the higher concentration of 40 mmol L−1 NaOH was selected, due to its stronger tolerance.

The reaction time and reaction temperature were investigated since both of them always influence on the CQDs-silver NPs formation. In Fig. 3D, the results showed a positive correlation between reaction time and the solution absorbance. At 30 min, the system absorbance reached constant, 30 min was selected as the reaction time, as prolonging the reaction time was unnecessary. As shown in Fig. 3E, the optimum reaction temperature was optimized at 50 °C. Therefore, the reaction time and temperature were set at 30 min and 50 °C in the following experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the colorimetric system
ce:section.ce:para: Melamine can cause aggregation in the CQDs-silver NPs, resulting in changes in solution color changes and absorbance (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4A showed that the absorbance of CQDs-silver NPs at 405 nm decreased after melamine addition, while a new band around 500 nm appeared which indicated an aggregation for CQDs-silver NPs. The results in Fig. 4B demonstrated that continuous addition of melamine into the CQDs-silver NPs, the solution color gradually faded and changed to red. The linearity of melamine with the solution absorbance (ΔR) was plotted ranging from 1 × 10−10 to 1 × 10−8 g/mL (y = 0.55 + 0.049x, R = 0.9596) and from 2.5 × 10−8 to 5 × 10−7 g/mL (y = 3.43 + 0.42x, R = 0.9961), respectively. The detection limit based on 3δ/k was low at 9.7 × 10−12 g/mL (eq. 65.3 pmol L−1). Additionally, the RSD values (n = 6) at four different concentrations of 5 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7, 1.5 × 10−7, and 2 × 10−7 g/mL was detected and determined to be 5.80, 1.90, 2.48, and 4.26%, respectively. The above results indicated that the proposed method possessed good sensitivity and repeatability.

Furthermore, the analytical merits of proposed method were compared with other reference on melamine detection (Table 1 ). The detection sensitivity of the proposed method was much higher than the reported work, which could satisfy the requirements for melamine detectionin food (2.5 mg/kg). In addition, the prepared CQDs-silver NPs could be kept at 4 °C without any color change for two months, indicating its excellent stability (Fig. S5). The developed sensor system can work at room temperature making it possibly employed for on-site detection. In addition, most of the reported sensor systems in Table 1 were carried out on the functionalized Ag nanoparticles. In this work, both the preparation of the Ag nanocomposite and the detection of melamine were simultaneously achieved under a mild reaction condition. Thus, the proposed method was a promising choice for melamine monitoring in milk and milk powder products.

To evaluate the selectivity of this sensor for melamine detection in milk samples, 17 kinds of amino acid were tested under the optimized conditions. Fig. 5 showed the solution color changes (Fig. 5A) and the absorbance (Fig. 5B) of the CQDs-silver NPs in the presence of melamine and other potential interferences. As shown, no color changes occurred in the presence of the 17 kinds amino acid, and just the addition of melamine could induce the solution color changing from light yellow to red (Fig. 5A). The solution absorbance could be monitored to precisely evaluate the sensor selectivity towards melamine, as the absorbance change was much larger than that of the interference. The reasons might be that higher affinity ability for Ag+ than other inference [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analysis of melamine in the milk sample

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, Lily flower-derived CQDs were exploited for the in situ reduction of Ag+ to form CQDs-Ag NPs. Melamine demonstrated a significant effect on the formation and dispersion/stabilization of CQDs-Ag NPs, resulting in the solution color changes. Based on these findings, a simple and ultrasensitive colorimetric method for the detection of melamine in milk samples was developed. The limit of detection was determined to be 7.9 × 10−12 g/mL (eq. 65.3 pmol L−1), which was much lower than the safety limits of the European Union (2.5 mg/kg). Real milk sample measurements supported the good applicability of the presented method for facile monitoring of melamine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.81673325, 81711540311, and 21705156), and the CAS Pioneer Hundred Talents Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.01.108.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wei-Feng Wang obtained his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 2015. Then, he joined Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China, as a lecturer. His research interests focus on developing fluorescent chemosensors and new method of capillary electrophoresis for natural drug screening.

Yin Qiang obtained her Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 2011. Then, she joined Lanzhou University as a lecturer. Her research interests focus on discovering new bioactive natural compounds form medicine plants.

Xian-Hua Meng obtained his Ph.D. degree from Institute of Materia Medica, Chinese Academy of Medical Scineces, PR China, in 2016. Then, he joined Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China, as a lecturer. His research interests focus on discovering new lead compound from natural medicine plant.

Jun-Li Yang received his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 2012. He is currently a professor Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on screening and discovering potential lead compounds based on natural products research and developing fluorescent chemosensors.

Yan-Ping Shi received his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 1996. He is currently a professor Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on discovering new high active drug from natural medicine plant, developing complex sample pretreatment and fluorescent chemosensors.
